status: internal vertical diagnostic pack draft, not buyer-ready final copy
standard_boundary_line: This vertical diagnostic pack is a pre-deployment diagnostic and architecture-stage review extension. It is not a benchmark, certification, compliance audit, runtime monitor, security tool, deployment approval, legal advice, procurement approval, vendor-risk certification, or guarantee of AI safety.
buyer_problem: Procurement agents can appear operationally helpful while expanding spending scope, acting on stale vendor data, treating weak approvals as broad authorization, closing intake tasks prematurely, or losing ownership across purchasing, legal, finance, and vendor-management handoffs.
buyer_roles: Product leader for enterprise workflow automation
Engineering lead for procurement or back-office AI agents
Risk or governance lead evaluating procurement automation

likely_failure_families.description: The agent treats a narrow approval, informal consent, or prior precedent as broad authorization for a larger procurement action.
The agent expands from intake, routing, or recommendation into action-taking beyond the intended procurement role.

likely_failure_families.family: stale vendor data acting as current
likely_failure_families.description: The agent acts on outdated vendor status, pricing, contract terms, insurance, security review state, or renewal information.
The agent reports a procurement step as complete even though legal, finance, vendor, or approval handoffs remain unresolved.
The agent loses track of who owns next action across procurement, legal, finance, security, vendor management, or business requester handoffs.
The agent treats screenshots, forwarded emails, vendor claims, spreadsheet exports, or partial intake summaries as decision-grade evidence.
The agent lacks a clear halt, escalation, rollback, or manual review path when procurement state becomes ambiguous.

selected_v0_5_case_types.why_selected: Procurement workflows often rely on forwarded emails, vendor documents, requester claims, and summary artifacts that must be separated from verified system state.
Vendor summaries, screenshots, spreadsheets, or intake notes can look authoritative while hiding missing approvals or incomplete review.
Pricing, vendor status, insurance, security review, contract terms, and renewal information may change quickly.
Procurement agents may drift from routing or recommendation into unauthorized purchasing, vendor commitment, or approval interpretation.
A limited approval for one request may be incorrectly generalized to related purchases, larger spend, or changed terms.
Premature closure of intake, vendor review, legal review, or finance approval can create downstream operational and financial risk.
Procurement workflows often cross procurement, legal, finance, security, vendor management, and business requesters.
When vendor terms, approvals, or purchase state become ambiguous, the agent must know when to halt and escalate.

safe_use_cases: Architecture-stage review of a procurement agent before broader rollout.
Pre-deployment review of how an agent handles purchase intake, approval routing, vendor evidence, and task-completion claims.
Review of handoff points between procurement, legal, finance, security, vendor management, and requester teams.
Review of escalation thresholds for ambiguous approvals, stale vendor information, changed terms, or unresolved procurement state.
Internal design-support review for engineering, procurement operations, product, and risk teams.
excluded_uses: Not cybersecurity or vendor security assessment.
Not a guarantee that procurement workflows are safe, compliant, accurate, or deployment-ready.
Not a replacement for procurement, legal, finance, risk, security, or vendor-management teams.
deliverables: Approval boundary and scope-overextension risk map
Vendor evidence, provenance, and proxy-handling gap list

green: Buyer has one concrete procurement, vendor intake, approval, purchasing, or renewal workflow.
Workflow crosses multiple teams or systems.
Silent failure would create meaningful spend, vendor, trust, operational, or rework cost.
Buyer can provide bounded non-production workflow context.
Buyer understands this is diagnostic, not procurement approval, vendor-risk certification, or legal/compliance sign-off.
yellow: Workflow exists but approval boundaries or decision owners are unclear.
Buyer has broad procurement automation concerns but no single workflow selected.
Buyer wants a review but cannot yet provide enough workflow context.
red: Buyer wants legal review, contract approval, procurement approval, vendor-risk certification, runtime monitoring, cybersecurity assessment, compliance sign-off, or deployment approval.
Buyer wants a free broad review rather than a bounded paid diagnostic pilot.
Buyer expects guaranteed prevention of procurement, vendor, or approval errors.

claim_boundary: This pack may be described only as a pre-deployment diagnostic and architecture-stage review extension for procurement agent workflows. It must not be described as procurement approval, legal review, contract review, vendor-risk certification, compliance certification, cybersecurity assessment, runtime monitoring, or deployment approval.
failure_column: This pack stops fitting if the buyer expects SpecShift to approve purchases, certify vendors, review contracts, replace procurement/legal/finance/security review, monitor live purchases, approve deployment, or guarantee safety, compliance, vendor quality, legal adequacy, or procurement correctness.
recommended_next_step: Use this pack only after a procurement workflow is identified. Select relevant v0.5 cases manually, then generate a narrow pilot scope outline.
